event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the region's interior savannas and forests, leaving early stone tools.
Archaic shell-midden cultures develop along the coast and rivers, exploiting fish and shellfish.
Early Amerindian groups begin cultivating cassava and other crops, marking a shift toward settled agriculture.
Arawak (Lokono) peoples establish farming villages across the coastal lowlands and riverbanks.
Carib peoples migrate into the region, competing with the Arawak for coastal and riverine territory.
Christopher Columbus sights the Guiana coast during his third voyage, the first recorded European contact.
Spanish explorer Alonso de Ojeda explores the Guiana coastline.
Sir Walter Raleigh explores the Orinoco region in search of the legendary city of El Dorado.
Dutch settlers establish a trading post at Kyk-Over-Al on the Essequibo River, beginning Dutch colonization.
The Dutch West India Company is chartered and takes control of colonization efforts in the Guianas.
The Dutch open the Demerara region to settlement, expanding sugar plantations along the coast.
Enslaved Africans led by Cuffy (Kofi) launch the Berbice Slave Rebellion against Dutch planters.
British forces seize the Dutch colonies of Demerara, Essequibo, and Berbice during the Anglo-Dutch War.
Britain occupies the Dutch Guiana colonies again amid the wars of the French Revolution.
The Anglo-Dutch Treaty formally cedes Demerara, Essequibo, and Berbice to Britain.
The Demerara Rebellion sees thousands of enslaved people rise up, brutally suppressed by colonial forces.
Britain unites the three colonies into the single Crown colony of British Guiana.
Slavery is abolished in British Guiana, followed by a four-year apprenticeship period.
Full emancipation takes effect and the first indentured laborers from India begin arriving to work the sugar estates.
Gold is discovered in the interior, sparking a mining boom and deeper penetration of the hinterland.
An international arbitration tribunal settles the Venezuela-British Guiana boundary largely in Britain's favor.
Indentured labor from India formally ends after bringing roughly 240,000 immigrants over nearly 80 years.
British Guiana becomes a Crown colony with a constitution reducing local self-government.
Cheddi Jagan and Forbes Burnham found the People's Progressive Party, launching the independence movement.
The PPP wins the first elections under universal suffrage, but Britain suspends the constitution and sends troops.
The PPP splits along ethnic lines as Forbes Burnham breaks away to later form the People's National Congress.
British Guiana gains internal self-government with Cheddi Jagan as premier.
Violent disturbances and a general strike erupt in Georgetown amid Cold War political tensions.
Inter-ethnic violence between Indo-Guyanese and Afro-Guyanese peaks before Forbes Burnham becomes premier.
British Guiana gains independence as Guyana on 26 May with Forbes Burnham as prime minister.
Guyana is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Guyana becomes a Cooperative Republic, severing ties to the British monarchy with a non-executive president.
Burnham's government nationalizes the bauxite and sugar industries, including the Booker McConnell estates.
Over 900 members of the Peoples Temple die in a mass murder-suicide at Jonestown in northwestern Guyana.
A new constitution establishes an executive presidency, and Forbes Burnham becomes president.
Historian and activist Walter Rodney is assassinated by a car bomb in Georgetown.
Forbes Burnham dies in office and is succeeded as president by Desmond Hoyte.
Cheddi Jagan and the PPP win the first internationally certified free elections since independence.
President Cheddi Jagan dies in office; his wife Janet Jagan is later elected president.
Bharrat Jagdeo becomes president, beginning a long PPP-led era of economic reform.
Catastrophic flooding devastates the coastal region, displacing tens of thousands and causing major economic loss.
Two mass shooting massacres at Lusignan and Bartica shock the nation amid a wave of violent crime.
ExxonMobil announces a major offshore oil discovery in the Stabroek Block, transforming Guyana's prospects.
David Granger leads the APNU+AFC coalition to election victory, ending 23 years of PPP rule.
Guyana takes the Venezuela border dispute to the International Court of Justice in The Hague.
First commercial crude oil is produced from the offshore Liza field, launching Guyana as an oil exporter.
A disputed, months-long election ends with Irfaan Ali and the PPP/C taking power after recounts.
Venezuela holds a referendum claiming the Essequibo region, sharply escalating tensions with Guyana.
Oil-driven growth makes Guyana one of the world's fastest-growing economies, dramatically expanding GDP.
